FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study (Year 2; N=9429); cross-sectional analysis of 11-12-year-old early adolescents from ABCD baseline

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures cyberbullying victimization and perpetration; abstract states 'Total screen time, particularly on the internet and social media, was associated with both cyberbullying victimization and perpetration.' Social media and internet are specifically measured SM variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple logistic regression analyses with sociodemographic factors as IVs and cyberbullying as DV; also reports logistic regression associating screen time modalities with cyberbullying outcomes. Table 3 shows adjusted OR for total screen time and specific modalities (social media, internet) with cyberbullying.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports OR with 95% CI for screen time associations with cyberbullying. Example: total screen time adjusted model reports 1.11 (95% CI 1.08-1.14) for victimization and 1.10 (95% CI 1.06-1.14) for perpetration per additional hour.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using ABCD Study baseline data; reports associations between sociodemographic factors and cyberbullying, plus associations between screen time (phone/SM) and cyberbullying outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics 2022; not a preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes smartphone/social media as independent variable predicting cyberbullying victimization/perpetration (non-SM outcomes). Additionally, paper reports associations with sociodemographic predictors but these are in cross-sectional design with phone/SM as IV, not as DV. Criterion (a) not met: phone/SM is not DV in every analysis.
notes: E6 applies: Paper measures cyberbullying separately AND also measures phone/SM specifically (internet and social media time associations with cyberbullying reported in Table 3). MET for FT-IC2 due to specific SM measurement.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study baseline data (2016-2018; N=10,755); cross-sectional analysis of 9-10-year-old children from ABCD.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures 6 contemporary screen time modalities: television, videos (YouTube), video games, social networking, texting, and video chat. Per criteria, TV viewing alone is NOT_MET (excluded modality); video games unspecified platform is NOT_MET; videos on non-phone devices is NOT_MET. While texting, social networking, and video chat are qualifying SM variables, the paper reports them only as part of a composite 'social media (social networking, video chat, texting)' measure in Table II and uses regression models that do not separately extract phone/SM-specific coefficients from the composite. Generic 'total screen time' composite with no phone/SM breakdown = NOT_MET per FT-IC2 rule.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses conducted to estimate associations between sociodemographic factors (sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parental education) and 6 contemporary forms of screen time. Test statistics (β coefficients) reported for each modality.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table III reports β coefficients with 95% CI for sociodemographic associations with screen time modalities. Example: Black children reported 1.58 (95% CI 1.35-1.79) more hours of screen time per day compared with White children.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using ABCD Study baseline data; reports associations between sociodemographic factors and contemporary screen time modalities.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Pediatrics 2022; not a preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions are met: (a) Screen time (DV) is outcome in all analyses; (b) Predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual (sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parental education) with no individual psychological/clinical variables; (c) Cross-sectional design. Paper does not report any individual psychological predictors (ADHD, depression, anxiety, etc.) and does not include phone/SM → non-SM outcome analyses. This is pure sociodemographic prediction of screen time in cross-sectional format, triggering FT-EC7 exclusion.
notes: E2 partially applies: Screen time is measured as multiple modalities including composite 'social media' but phone/SM-specific row/column is not separately extractable for independent statistical test; reported only as composite. Paper fails FT-IC2 due to inability to extract phone/SM-specific coefficient from composite screen time measures. FT-EC7 applies: excludes this paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study COVID-19 survey data (December 2020; N=4,106); adolescents ages 12-14 from ABCD cohort.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures exposure to COVID-19 information on social media (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, TikTok, SnapChat), television media, and internet. Adolescents self-reported frequency of checking COVID-19 information on social media and television. Social media is a specific SM variable per FT-IC2 qualifying criteria.

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses with coefficients transformed to risk ratios. Table 2 reports associations between COVID-19 information sources (social media, television, internet) and masking behavior outcomes, adjusted for confounders.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports RR and ARR with 95% CI. Example: Adjusted RR for checking COVID-related social media activity 1.09 (95% CI 1.00-1.19); View COVID television media coverage ARR 1.09 (95% CI 1.01-1.17); Parent counseling ARR 1.28 (95% CI 1.20-1.36).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using ABCD COVID-19 survey data; reports associations between multiple information sources (social media, television, internet, parental counseling) and masking behavior.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Preventive Medicine Reports 2022; not a preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Condition (b) is not met: Predictors include parental counseling (behavioral/contextual) and multiple information sources (social media, television, internet), which are not purely demographic. The paper reports masking as DV with heterogeneous predictor types (not demographic-only). Additionally, paper is cross-sectional which would require demographic-only predictors for EC7 to apply; since predictors are mixed types, EC7 criterion (b) fails.
notes: Paper meets all FT-IC1 through FT-IC6 criteria. FT-EC7 does not apply because the predictors are not limited to demographic/contextual factors only - they include behavioral/informational exposure variables (social media, TV, internet, parental counseling). Paper is INCLUDE.
